title: B.2 — Implementation Guidance: Policies for AI
content: B.2.1 Objective: Provide management direction and support for AI systems according to business requirements. B.2.2 AI Policy: The AI policy should be informed by business strategy, organizational values, risk levels, legal requirements, risk environment, and impact to interested parties. It should include principles guiding all AI activities and processes for handling deviations. Should cross-reference policies on AI resources, impact assessments, and AI system development. B.2.3 Alignment with other policies: Determine where quality, security, safety, and privacy policies intersect with AI objectives. Update or provide cross-references. B.2.4 Review of AI policy: A management-approved role should review and evaluate the AI policy, assessing opportunities for improvement in response to organizational, business, legal, or technical changes.

title: B.3 — Implementation Guidance: Internal Organization
content: B.3.1 Objective: Establish accountability for responsible AI implementation, operation, and management. B.3.2 AI Roles and Responsibilities: Define roles for risk management, AI impact assessments, asset/resource management, security, safety, privacy, development, performance, human oversight, supplier relationships, legal compliance, and data quality management throughout the whole life cycle. Responsibilities should match individuals' levels. B.3.3 Reporting of Concerns: Implement a reporting mechanism with: confidentiality/anonymity options, availability to employees and contractors, qualified investigation staff, appropriate investigation powers, timely escalation to management, protection from reprisals, responses within appropriate timeframes. See also ISO 37002 for whistleblowing guidance.

title: B.4 — Implementation Guidance: Resources for AI Systems
content: B.4.1 Objective: Account for all AI system resources to understand and address risks and impacts. B.4.2 Resource Documentation: Document resources at each lifecycle stage including AI system components, data resources, tooling resources, system/computing resources, and human resources. Resources can be provided by the organization, customers, or third parties. B.4.3 Data Resources: Document provenance, update dates, data categories (training/validation/test/production), labelling processes, intended use, quality, retention policies, known biases, and data preparation steps. B.4.4 Tooling Resources: Document algorithm types, ML models, data conditioning tools, optimization methods, evaluation methods, provisioning tools, and software/hardware. B.4.5 System and Computing Resources: Document compute requirements, location (on-prem/cloud/edge), processing resources, and environmental impact. B.4.6 Human Resources: Document data scientists, human oversight roles, safety/security/privacy experts, AI researchers, and domain experts needed at each lifecycle stage.

title: B.5 — Implementation Guidance: Assessing Impacts of AI Systems
content: B.5.1 Objective: Assess AI system impacts to individuals, groups, and societies throughout the lifecycle. B.5.2 Impact Assessment Process: Consider whether the AI system affects legal position, physical/psychological well-being, universal human rights, or societies. Define circumstances for assessment (criticality, complexity, data sensitivity), assessment elements (identification of sources/events/outcomes, consequence/likelihood analysis, acceptance decisions, treatment measures, documentation/reporting), who performs it, how results inform design/use, and potentially impacted groups. B.5.3 Documentation: Retain impact assessment results documenting intended use, foreseeable misuse, positive/negative impacts, predictable failures, relevant demographic groups, system complexity, human oversight roles, and employment impacts. B.5.4 Individual Impact: Assess fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability, security, privacy, safety, financial consequences, accessibility, and human rights. B.5.5 Societal Impact: Assess environmental sustainability (including greenhouse emissions), economic impact, government/democratic processes, health and safety, cultural norms and values.

title: B.6 — Implementation Guidance: AI System Lifecycle
content: B.6.1 Management Guidance for AI Development: B.6.1.2 Objectives for responsible development — integrate fairness, safety, transparency into requirements, data acquisition, model training, verification, and validation. B.6.1.3 Responsible development processes — consider lifecycle stages, testing requirements, human oversight, impact assessment timing, training data rules, expertise needed, release criteria, approvals, change control, usability, and stakeholder engagement. B.6.2 AI System Lifecycle Controls: B.6.2.2 Requirements/Specification — document rationale, goals, data requirements. B.6.2.3 Design/Development Documentation — ML approach, learning algorithms, data quality, hardware/software, security threats (data poisoning, model stealing, inversion attacks), human interaction, interoperability. B.6.2.4 Verification/Validation — testing methodologies, evaluation criteria, plan for risk assessment. B.6.2.5 Deployment — deployment plan with release criteria, user testing, management approvals. B.6.2.6 Operation/Monitoring — performance monitoring, data drift detection, repairs, updates, support, security threats. B.6.2.7 Technical Documentation — purpose, usage instructions, assumptions, limitations, monitoring capabilities, architecture, design choices, data info, risk management records. B.6.2.8 Event Logs — automatic logging for traceability and performance detection, retain per data retention policies.

title: B.7 — Implementation Guidance: Data for AI Systems
content: B.7.1 Objective: Ensure the organization understands data's role and impacts throughout AI lifecycle. B.7.2 Data Management: Address privacy/security implications, security/safety threats, transparency/explainability (data provenance, how data determines outputs), representativeness vs operational domain, accuracy and integrity. B.7.3 Data Acquisition: Document categories needed, quantity, sources (internal/purchased/shared/open/synthetic), source characteristics (static/streamed/gathered/generated), data subject demographics/biases, prior handling, data rights (PII, copyright), metadata, and provenance. B.7.4 Data Quality: Quality has significant impact on output validity (ISO/IEC 25024). For supervised/semi-supervised ML, define and measure quality of training/validation/test/production data. Consider bias impact on performance and fairness, make adjustments as necessary. B.7.5 Data Provenance: Record creation, update, transcription, abstraction, validation, transfer, and sharing history per ISO 8000-2. Verify provenance measures based on source and context. B.7.6 Data Preparation: Document criteria for selecting preparation methods including statistical exploration, cleaning, imputation, normalization, scaling, labelling, and encoding.

title: B.8 — Implementation Guidance: Information for Interested Parties
content: B.8.1 Objective: Ensure interested parties have necessary information to understand and assess AI risks and impacts. B.8.2 System Documentation for Users: Provide purpose, notification of AI interaction, how to interact, how/when to override, technical requirements, limitations, expected lifetime, human oversight needs, accuracy/performance info, impact assessment results, revision claims, update schedules, contact info, educational materials. Document criteria for what information to provide based on intended use, foreseeable misuse, expertise, and specific impact. B.8.3 External Reporting: Provide capabilities for users/external parties to report adverse impacts (e.g., unfairness). B.8.4 Communication of Incidents: Plan for communicating AI-specific and security/privacy incidents, considering notification timelines, authorities to notify, and required details. Integrate with broader incident management per ISO 27001/ISO 27701. B.8.5 Information for Interested Parties: Document obligations for reporting to authorities/regulators including technical documentation, data sets, algorithmic choices, verification records, risks, impact assessments, logs.

title: B.9 — Implementation Guidance: Use of AI Systems
content: B.9.1 Objective: Ensure responsible AI use per organizational policies. B.9.2 Responsible Use Processes: Define processes for determining whether to use AI systems, considering required approvals, cost (including ongoing monitoring/maintenance), approved sourcing requirements, and applicable legal requirements. B.9.3 Responsible Use Objectives: Identify objectives including fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability, reliability, safety, robustness/redundancy, privacy/security, and accessibility. Implement human oversight mechanisms: human reviewers with authority to override AI decisions, ensuring oversight is included per deployment instructions, monitoring performance/accuracy, reporting concerns about outputs and impacts, considering whether automated decision-making is appropriate. Human oversight needs should be informed by AI system impact assessments. B.9.4 Intended Use: Deploy according to instructions and documentation. Ensure human oversight is applied as required. Monitor operation per B.6.2.6. Keep event logs demonstrating intended use.

title: B.10 — Implementation Guidance: Third-Party and Customer Relationships
content: B.10.1 Objective: Ensure the organization understands its responsibilities and remains accountable when third parties are involved. B.10.2 Allocating Responsibilities: Document all parties involved in the AI lifecycle (data providers, algorithm providers, developers, users, accountable parties) and determine their responsibilities. When supplying AI to third parties, ensure responsible development approach and provide necessary documentation (B.6.2.7, B.8.2). When PII is involved, consider PII controller/processor roles per ISO/IEC 29100 and ISO/IEC 27701. B.10.3 Suppliers: Establish processes to ensure supplier services/products/materials align with the organization's responsible AI approach. Consider different supplier types, risk levels, selection requirements, and ongoing monitoring/evaluation. Require suppliers to deliver appropriate documentation and take corrective actions if performance causes adverse impacts. B.10.4 Customers: Understand customer expectations for responsible AI. These can come as product requirements, contractual requirements, or usage agreements. Understand where responsibility lies between provider and customer.

title: Annex C — Potential AI-Related Organizational Objectives and Risk Sources
content: Annex C (informative) provides examples of organizational objectives and risk sources to support AI risk assessment (Clause 6.1). Organizational AI objectives can include: ensuring AI system reliability, ensuring fairness and non-discrimination, maintaining transparency and explainability, protecting privacy and data, ensuring safety and security, supporting human oversight, achieving accountability, and managing environmental impact. Risk sources can include: data quality issues (bias, incompleteness, errors), model limitations (overfitting, lack of generalization, adversarial vulnerability), operational risks (drift, degradation, dependency failures), organizational risks (inadequate governance, skill gaps, unclear accountability), external risks (regulatory changes, societal expectations, technological disruption), and third-party risks (supplier reliability, model provenance, integration complexity).

title: Annex D — Use of the AI Management System Across Domains or Sectors
content: Annex D (informative) provides guidance on applying the AIMS across sectors. The standard is applicable to any organization regardless of size, type, or sector. Different sectors may have specific AI regulations, risk profiles, and stakeholder expectations. Examples: Healthcare AI (high safety requirements, regulatory approval needs, patient data privacy), Financial Services AI (algorithmic trading, credit scoring, fraud detection — fairness and explainability critical), Public Sector AI (transparency, accountability, citizen rights), Manufacturing AI (safety components, quality control), Transportation AI (autonomous vehicles, safety-critical systems). Organizations should adapt the AIMS scope, risk criteria, and controls to their sector-specific context.

title: Annex A.2 Controls — Policies for AI (A.2.2, A.2.3, A.2.4)
content: A.2.2 AI Policy: The organization shall document a policy for the development or use of AI systems. A.2.3 Alignment with other organizational policies: The organization shall determine where other policies can be affected by or apply to the organization's AI objectives. A.2.4 Review of the AI policy: The AI policy shall be reviewed at planned intervals or as needed to ensure continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness.

title: Annex A.3 Controls — Internal Organization (A.3.2, A.3.3)
content: A.3.2 AI roles and responsibilities: Roles and responsibilities for AI shall be defined and allocated according to the needs of the organization. A.3.3 Reporting of concerns: The organization shall define and put in place a process to report concerns about the organization's role with respect to an AI system throughout its life cycle.

title: Annex A.4 Controls — Resources for AI Systems (A.4.2-A.4.6)
content: A.4.2 Resource documentation: Identify and document relevant resources at each AI lifecycle stage. A.4.3 Data resources: Document information about data resources utilized for the AI system. A.4.4 Tooling resources: Document information about tooling resources (algorithms, models, tools). A.4.5 System and computing resources: Document system and computing resources (hardware, cloud, storage). A.4.6 Human resources: Document human resources and their competences for development, deployment, operation, change management, maintenance, transfer, decommissioning, verification, and integration.

title: Annex A.5 Controls — Assessing Impacts (A.5.2-A.5.5)
content: A.5.2 AI system impact assessment process: Establish a process to assess potential consequences for individuals, groups, and societies throughout the AI system lifecycle. A.5.3 Documentation of AI system impact assessments: Document assessment results and retain for a defined period. A.5.4 Assessing AI system impact on individuals or groups: Assess and document potential impacts to individuals or groups throughout the system's lifecycle. A.5.5 Assessing societal impacts: Assess and document potential societal impacts (environmental sustainability, economic, government, health/safety, cultural norms/values) throughout their lifecycle.

title: Annex A.6 Controls — AI System Lifecycle (A.6.1.2-A.6.2.8)
content: A.6.1.2 Objectives for responsible development: Identify and document objectives to guide responsible AI development across the lifecycle. A.6.1.3 Processes for responsible design and development: Define and document specific processes for responsible AI design and development. A.6.2.2 AI system requirements and specification: Specify and document requirements for new or enhanced AI systems. A.6.2.3 Documentation of AI system design and development: Document design and development based on organizational objectives and specification criteria. A.6.2.4 AI system verification and validation: Define and document verification/validation measures and criteria. A.6.2.5 AI system deployment: Document a deployment plan ensuring requirements are met prior to deployment. A.6.2.6 AI system operation and monitoring: Define and document elements for ongoing operation (monitoring, repairs, updates, support). A.6.2.7 AI system technical documentation: Determine and provide technical documentation for each relevant category of interested parties. A.6.2.8 AI system recording of event logs: Determine lifecycle phases where event logging should be enabled, at minimum when the AI system is in use.

title: Annex A.7 Controls — Data for AI Systems (A.7.2-A.7.6)
content: A.7.2 Data for development and enhancement of AI system: Define, document, and implement data management processes. A.7.3 Acquisition of data: Determine and document details about data acquisition and selection. A.7.4 Quality of data for AI systems: Define and document requirements for data quality and ensure data meets those requirements. A.7.5 Data provenance: Define and document a process for recording provenance over data and AI system life cycles. A.7.6 Data preparation: Define and document criteria for selecting data preparations and methods to be used.

title: Annex A.8 Controls — Information for Interested Parties (A.8.2-A.8.5)
content: A.8.2 System documentation and information for users: Determine and provide necessary information to system users. A.8.3 External reporting: Provide capabilities for interested parties to report adverse impacts. A.8.4 Communication of incidents: Determine and document a plan for communicating incidents to users. A.8.5 Information for interested parties: Determine and document obligations for reporting information about the AI system to interested parties (regulators, authorities).

title: Annex A.9 Controls — Use of AI Systems (A.9.2-A.9.4)
content: A.9.2 Processes for responsible use of AI systems: Define and document processes for responsible use, including approvals, cost considerations, sourcing requirements, and legal requirements. A.9.3 Objectives for responsible use of AI system: Identify and document objectives (fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability, reliability, safety, robustness, privacy/security, accessibility) and implement human oversight mechanisms. A.9.4 Intended use of the AI system: Ensure the AI system is used according to intended uses and accompanying documentation.

title: Annex A.10 Controls — Third-Party and Customer Relationships (A.10.2-A.10.4)
content: A.10.2 Allocating responsibilities: Ensure responsibilities within the AI system lifecycle are allocated between the organization, partners, suppliers, customers, and third parties. A.10.3 Suppliers: Establish a process to ensure supplier services/products/materials align with the organization's responsible AI approach. A.10.4 Customers: Ensure the responsible approach to AI development and use considers customer expectations and needs.

title: Article 17 — Quality Management System for High-Risk AI
content: Providers of high-risk AI systems shall put a quality management system in place. The QMS shall include: a strategy for regulatory compliance, techniques and procedures for design, development, quality control and assurance, examination/test/validation procedures, technical specifications, systems and procedures for data management, the risk management system, post-market monitoring, procedures for reporting serious incidents, communication with competent authorities, recordkeeping, resource management, and an accountability framework. The QMS should be proportionate to the size of the provider's organization.

title: Article 26 — Obligations of Deployers of High-Risk AI
content: Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall: use the system in accordance with instructions of use, ensure human oversight by persons with competence/training/authority, ensure input data is relevant and sufficiently representative, monitor operation, inform the provider about serious incidents or malfunctions, perform a fundamental rights impact assessment for public entities, and keep logs generated by the system for a period appropriate to the intended purpose (at least 6 months). Deployers using high-risk AI in the workplace shall inform workers' representatives and affected workers.

title: Article 27 — Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment for High-Risk AI
content: Before deploying a high-risk AI system, deployers that are bodies governed by public law or private entities providing public services shall perform an assessment of the impact on fundamental rights. The assessment shall include: a description of the deployer's processes involving the high-risk AI system, a description of the period and frequency of use, categories of natural persons and groups likely affected, specific risks of harm, description of human oversight measures, measures to be taken if risks materialize, and governance and complaint mechanisms.

title: NIST AI RMF — Key Subcategories for OCI Compliance
content: GOVERN 1.1: Legal/regulatory requirements are identified. GOVERN 1.2: Trustworthy AI characteristics are integrated into policies. GOVERN 1.3: Processes for AI risk are established. GOVERN 1.5: Ongoing monitoring of risk management. GOVERN 2.1: Roles/responsibilities defined. GOVERN 2.2: Mechanisms for feedback on AI risk management. GOVERN 4.1: Organizational practices for AI transparency. GOVERN 6.1: Policies for third-party AI. MAP 1.1: Intended purpose/context documented. MAP 1.5: Organizational risk tolerances. MAP 2.1: Data requirements documented. MAP 2.3: Scientific integrity and TEVV practices. MAP 5.1: Likelihood and impact of benefits/harms. MEASURE 1.1: Approaches for measurement. MEASURE 2.1: AI system evaluated for valid/reliable. MEASURE 2.5: AI evaluated for security/resilience. MANAGE 1.1: AI risk treatment prioritized. MANAGE 2.3: AI risk responses deployed. MANAGE 2.4: Mechanisms for risk monitoring. MANAGE 4.1: Post-deployment monitoring. MANAGE 4.2: Performance review.

title: CIS OCI Section 2 — Networking
content: Key CIS OCI Networking recommendations: 2.1 Ensure no security lists allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22 (SSH); 2.2 Ensure no security lists allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389 (RDP); 2.3 Ensure no network security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22; 2.4 Ensure no network security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389; 2.5 Ensure VCN flow logging is enabled for all subnets; 2.6 Ensure at least one Service Gateway is configured. These controls protect AI system infrastructure from unauthorized network access.

title: CIS OCI Section 5 — Asset Management
content: Key CIS OCI Asset Management recommendations: 5.1 Ensure default tags are used on resources for resource tracking; 5.2 Ensure Oracle Analytics Cloud instances are not publicly accessible; 5.3 Ensure Oracle Integration Cloud instances are not publicly accessible. Tags enable AI system inventory tracking and cost allocation per ISO 42001 A.4 resource documentation requirements.

title: OCI Data Science Service — ISO 42001 Compliance Mapping
content: OCI Data Science maps to ISO 42001 controls: A.4.4 (tooling resources — notebook sessions, model catalog), A.4.5 (computing resources — shapes, GPU instances), A.6.2.2 (requirements — project documentation), A.6.2.3 (design documentation — model metadata/descriptions), A.6.2.4 (verification — model version sets for A/B testing), A.6.2.5 (deployment — model deployments with endpoints), A.6.2.6 (monitoring — deployment logging, access/predict logs), A.6.2.7 (technical docs — model catalog provenance metadata), A.6.2.8 (event logs — category log details for access/predict). Alternative tools: AWS SageMaker, Azure ML Studio, Google Vertex AI, MLflow, Kubeflow, or any ML platform.

title: OCI Generative AI Service — ISO 42001 Compliance Mapping
content: OCI Generative AI maps to ISO 42001 controls: A.4.4 (tooling resources — base models, fine-tuned models), A.6.2.3 (design — model type documentation), A.6.2.5 (deployment — endpoints), A.10.3 (suppliers — approved base models from Oracle partnerships). For EU AI Act compliance, GenAI usage may trigger GPAI obligations (Art.53) if fine-tuned models are provided to third parties. Risk tier classification depends on use case, not the model itself. Alternative tools: Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Hugging Face, or self-hosted open-source LLMs.

title: OCI Cloud Guard — ISO 42001 Compliance Mapping
content: OCI Cloud Guard maps to ISO 42001 controls: A.5.2 (impact assessment — security problem detection), Cl.6 (planning — risk identification via detector recipes), Cl.9 (performance evaluation — security scoring trends), Cl.10 (improvement — recommendations and responder recipes for auto-remediation). Cloud Guard provides continuous security posture management for AI infrastructure. Alternative CSPM tools: Wiz, Prisma Cloud, Orca Security, AWS SecurityHub, Azure Defender, or open-source tools like Prowler.

title: OCI IAM — ISO 42001 Compliance Mapping
content: OCI IAM maps to ISO 42001 controls: A.2.2 (AI policy — IAM policy statements for AI services), A.3.2 (roles — IAM groups for AI administrators), A.9.2/A.9.3 (responsible use — group-scoped access, MFA enforcement), Cl.5 (leadership — tenancy admin groups, policy governance). IAM is a requirement-level control (not recommendation) because access governance is fundamental to any AIMS regardless of cloud provider. Alternative: Any cloud IAM (AWS IAM, Azure AD/Entra ID, Google Cloud IAM) or identity providers (Okta, Ping, CyberArk).

title: Related AI Standards Referenced by ISO 42001
content: ISO/IEC 22989:2022 — AI concepts and terminology (normative reference). ISO/IEC 23894:2023 — AI risk management guidance. ISO/IEC 38507:2022 — Governance implications of the use of AI by organizations (governing body guidance). ISO/IEC 5338 — AI system lifecycle processes. ISO/IEC 23053 — Machine learning framework. ISO/IEC 25024 — Data quality model. ISO/IEC 5259 series — Data quality for analytics and ML. ISO/IEC 24028:2020 — Overview of trustworthiness in AI. ISO/IEC 24029-1 — Robustness of neural networks. ISO/IEC TR 24368 — Ethical and societal concerns in AI. ISO 37002 — Whistleblowing management systems. ISO 9241-210 — Human-centred design. ISO/IEC 27001 — Information security management. ISO/IEC 27701 — Privacy information management.
